title: He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’
scripture_text: “Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.
devotional:
2. Every beatitude is in striking contrast to the character and conduct of the Scribes and Pharisees. When you look at the attitudes portrayed by the scribes and the Pharisees, it was completely opposite to that described by Jesus in the beatitudes. Twice in the sermon Jesus warns his disciples "do not be like them" Matthew 6:5,8. Notice carefully what Jesus says here, He did not say, "Do not do what they do," but "do not be like them." He was referring to their characters.

The Pharisees were proud, not poor in spirit.

There are two Greek words for poor. PENES describe the person who does not have a lot; they live from day to day, someone like a day labourer, who was paid a daily wage. The second word is the word PTOCHOS which means to crouch like a beggar, this describes the person who has nothing, who is completely destitute, and this is the word that Jesus uses. Therefore, being poor in spirit is a strong realisation of our desperate need of God, not just for salvation, but for everything in life.

The Pharisees were self-satisfied self-absorbed, and self-righteous. Because of their self-righteousness they had nothing to mourn over. When Jesus speaks of mourning he uses a very strong word. It was the word used to describe the external evidences of an internal grief. In other words, Jesus is talking about people being real. Often in Christian circles there is a tendency to bury how we really feel in order to protect ourselves from further pain or rejection. But Jesus is saying that mourning is part of a healthy Christian experience.

When we bury stuff we die spiritually, many people believe that if they ignore a problem, or bury their pain it will go away. Living in the kingdom of God is a call to reality.

The focus of the Pharisees was always to keep up the appearance of have everything under control, to look cool, but never dealing with reality. Those who mourn will find comfort; the comfort of God that brings healing and hope.

This is a very important principle for us to learn, there is great temptation for us to want to look cool, and in the process bury wounds and hurts that can only be healed when they are brought out in the open.

The Pharisees were hard, quick to condemn others. They were not gentle or meek.

The biblical word "meek" comes from the Greek word praos, which means "mild, humble, or gentle." Classical Greek uses it in many ways. Meekness refers to describe a soothing medicine. Meekness refers to a gentle breeze. Meekness refers to a colt that is broken and useful. Meekness refers to person who treats others with respect, dignity, politeness and courtesy. The word for meekness was used in much extra-biblical literature to refer to a horse that had been trained. Therefore the idea is "power under control."A horse that is trained is useful and safe. However one that cannot be controlled is useless and dangerous. It was used to describe a medicine that can bring healing.

However a horse that cannot be controlled can kill. A person who can control his words is encouraging and pleasant. A person who is not meek and gentle is out of control. Living in the kingdom of God is bowing to the control of another.

"The meek will inherit the earth." There is a simple principle here; God will not give those who are out of control any inheritance. God gives those who are gentle, positions of authority.

But how is such meekness produced in a person? This gentleness is the result of the healing comfort of God. In the beatitude before this one we are told that those who mourn shall be comforted. The word for comfort means to draw along beside someone in order to encourage and exhort him or her in times of sorrow. Often people end up hard and bitter as a result of the sorrows of life. This hardness is caused by the refusal to receive the comfort of God.
prayer: Prayer, Father is there any area of my life that is not touched by meekness, where I am out of control?
html:
He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’

Matthew 5:1-12 The Beatitudes Part 2.
“Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.

2. Every beatitude is in striking contrast to the character and conduct of the Scribes and Pharisees. When you look at the attitudes portrayed by the scribes and the Pharisees, it was completely opposite to that described by Jesus in the beatitudes. But how is such meekness produced in a person? This gentleness is the result of the healing comfort of God. In the beatitude before this one we are told that those who mourn shall be comforted. The word for comfort means to draw along beside someone in order to encourage and exhort him or her in times of sorrow. Often people end up hard and bitter as a result of the sorrows of life. This hardness is caused by the refusal to receive the comfort of God.

Prayer: Prayer, Father is there any area of my life that is not touched by meekness, where I am out of control?
Bible in a year reading plan: - Judges 14-16
